lorebook.name: Dunwater Coast
lorebook.description: Dunwater Coast - A world of island chains, open ocean, and port towns that ask no questions. Pirates, trade, and salt.

entries.name: [Meta] NPC Naming Convention
entries.content: When generating new NPCs on the Dunwater Coast, use names that belong to the sea — earned at sea, given by storms, taken from the trade. Examples: Corsair, Brine, Magistra Fenn, Tallow, Sirocco, Pell.
entries.description: NPC Naming Convention. When generating new NPCs on the Dunwater Coast, use names that belong to the sea — earned at sea, given by storms, taken from the trade.

entries.name: [Character] Corsair — Captain
entries.content:
Corsair captains the Blacktide — a ship that has been repaired so many times nothing original remains. Charismatic, ruthless when needed, loyal to crew above all else. Missing two fingers on the left hand. Doesn't talk about how. Smells like salt and tar.

When Corsair speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#4682B4"> tags.

<font color="#4682B4">"The sea doesn't care who you were. It cares what you can do right now. So — what can you do?"</font>

<BunnymoTags>Corsair: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:30s>, <BUILD:Weathered>, <TRAIT:Charismatic>, <TRAIT:Ruthless>, <TRAIT:Loyal></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Corsair captains the Blacktide — a ship that has been repaired so many times nothing original remains.

entries.name: [Character] Magistra Fenn — Harbormaster
entries.content:
Magistra Fenn controls who docks at the main port and who doesn't. Bureaucratic, incorruptible, feared by captains who owe fees. Keeps meticulous records in a leather-bound ledger. Has never been seen outside the harbor office. Some say she sleeps there. Some say she doesn't sleep.

When Fenn speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#5F9EA0"> tags.

<font color="#5F9EA0">"Docking fee is twelve silver. Manifest inspection is mandatory. Complaints can be filed in writing. I do not accept them verbally."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Magistra Fenn: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:50s>, <BUILD:Straight-backed>, <TRAIT:Bureaucratic>, <TRAIT:Incorruptible>, <TRAIT:Feared></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Magistra Fenn controls who docks at the main port and who doesn't. Bureaucratic, incorruptible, feared by captains who owe fees.

entries.name: [Character] Sirocco — Navigator
entries.content:
Sirocco navigates by the sky — stars, clouds, wind patterns, things others can't see. Quiet, reads the weather like a book. Claims to have sailed to the edge of the world and found nothing there. Doesn't elaborate. Her maps are drawn from memory and they are always right.

When Sirocco speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#87CEEB"> tags.

<font color="#87CEEB">"Storm coming. Three hours, maybe four. We should be past the shoals by then. If we leave now."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Sirocco: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:Late 20s>, <BUILD:Lean>, <TRAIT:Quiet>, <TRAIT:Perceptive>, <TRAIT:Certain></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Sirocco navigates by the sky — stars, clouds, wind patterns, things others can't see.

entries.name: [Overview] Dunwater Coast
entries.content: Dunwater Coast is a world of island chains, open ocean, and port towns built on the understanding that everyone here is running from something and that the sea is generous enough to let them. The islands number in the hundreds — volcanic peaks, coral atolls, jungle-covered landmasses — scattered across an ocean that is vast, deep, and not entirely mapped. Port towns cluster on natural harbors and make their living from trade, smuggling, and the practical difference between the two, which is mostly a matter of paperwork. The water is warm, the storms are violent, and the culture is built on salt, wind, and the specific kind of trust that develops between people who share a small boat on a large ocean.
entries.description: Dunwater Coast is a world of island chains, open ocean, and port towns built on the understanding that everyone here is running from…

entries.name: [Overview] Seafaring Culture
entries.content: Life on the Dunwater Coast is life on the water. Children learn to swim before they walk and to sail before they can read. The ocean is workplace, highway, and defining feature of every conversation. Sailors develop a vocabulary for water conditions that distinguishes between dozens of states of choppiness, wind direction, and current behavior. Boats are personal in a way that borders on sacred — a captain's relationship with their vessel is the most important relationship in their life, and suggesting otherwise is grounds for a fight. The sea provides: fish, trade routes, escape, and the kind of freedom that comes from being in a place where the law's jurisdiction ends at the waterline.
entries.description: Seafaring Culture. Life on the Dunwater Coast is life on the water. Children learn to swim before they walk and to sail before they can read.

entries.name: [Overview] The Economy of Salt
entries.content: The Dunwater Coast's economy is trade, and trade here means moving goods between islands by ship. Legal trade — spices, timber, textiles, preserved food — flows through port towns where magistrates collect tariffs that fund basic governance. Illegal trade — everything else — flows through every other point on the coastline. The line between legal and illegal is negotiable, situational, and frequently blurred by captains who carry both types of cargo on the same ship. Money matters, but reputation matters more: a captain known for delivering cargo intact and on time will never lack for work, regardless of what the cargo is. The economy runs on trust between people who cannot afford to be naive about each other.
entries.description: The Economy of Salt. The Dunwater Coast's economy is trade, and trade here means moving goods between islands by ship.

entries.name: [Overview] Why Everyone Is Running
entries.content: The Dunwater Coast attracts people who need to be somewhere else — somewhere that the past cannot reach, where the name you give is the name you have, and where the only thing that matters is what you can do today. Sailors, merchants, captains, dockworkers — most arrived from somewhere with a story they do not tell and a reason they do not share. The coast does not ask. It understands that the ocean offers a particular kind of erasure: the person you were before you boarded the ship matters less with every mile of water between you and the place you left. This produces a culture of deliberate forgetting that is, paradoxically, deeply honest — people here are truthful about who they are now because they have been allowed to stop being who they were.
entries.description: Why Everyone Is Running. The Dunwater Coast attracts people who need to be somewhere else — somewhere that the past cannot reach, where the name you give is the…

entries.name: [Location] Saltmere — Major Port Town
entries.content: Saltmere is the largest port on the Dunwater Coast — a sprawling harbor town built on a natural deepwater bay on the largest island in the chain. The town curves around the bay in a crescent of docks, warehouses, taverns, and the kind of shops that cater to people with salt-crusted pockets and immediate needs. The harbor holds everything from single-mast fishing boats to multi-deck trading vessels, and the docks are busy at every hour. Saltmere has a magistrate, a customs house, and the thinnest pretense of order — the magistrate collects tariffs, the customs house records cargo, and everyone involved understands that what is recorded and what actually passes through are related but not identical.
entries.description: Saltmere is the largest port on the Dunwater Coast — a sprawling harbor town built on a natural deepwater bay on the largest island in the…

entries.name: [Location] The Pirate Haven — Blackpool
entries.content: Blackpool is Dunwater Coast's open secret — a port town on a volcanic island where no magistrate has ever successfully maintained authority. The harbor is sheltered by a natural breakwater of volcanic rock, and the town is built into the cliffsides above it, accessible by stairs carved into the stone. Blackpool is where pirate crews refit, resupply, and spend their earnings. It operates on its own rules: no violence in the market, honor ship-to-ship agreements, and pay what you owe. These rules are enforced by the captains' council — an informal assembly of the most established pirate crews who maintain order through mutual self-interest. Blackpool is not lawless. It is lawful on its own terms, and those terms are surprisingly functional.
entries.description: The Pirate Haven — Blackpool. Blackpool is Dunwater Coast's open secret — a port town on a volcanic island where no magistrate has ever successfully maintained authority.

entries.name: [Location] The Trading Post — Tidebreak
entries.content: Tidebreak is a neutral trading island positioned at the intersection of the coast's major shipping routes. The island itself is small — barely enough land for the market and the warehouses that surround it. Tidebreak exists for trade and nothing else: there are no permanent residents, only merchants who rotate through on seasonal schedules. The market is open-air, sheltered by canvas awnings, and stocked with goods from every island in the chain. Prices at Tidebreak set prices everywhere else. Disputes at Tidebreak are settled by arbiters who are paid by both parties and whose neutrality is their only marketable asset. Violence at Tidebreak results in permanent exclusion from the market, which is a commercial death sentence.
entries.description: The Trading Post — Tidebreak. Tidebreak is a neutral trading island positioned at the intersection of the coast's major shipping routes.

entries.name: [Location] The Open Sea
entries.content: The open sea between the Dunwater Coast's islands is the world's defining feature — vast, deep, and commanding respect from everyone who sails it. The water is warm on the surface and cold beneath, with currents that run between islands in patterns that experienced sailors read like roads. The sea provides fish in abundance, which feeds the coast's population. It also provides storms that arrive fast and hit hard, waves that can swamp a poorly handled vessel, and depths that contain things the fishermen do not discuss in detail. The open sea is beautiful in the specific way that dangerous things are beautiful — the blue is deeper than any paint, the sunsets are extraordinary, and the calm days make you forget the bad ones, which is the sea's most effective trick.
entries.description: The open sea between the Dunwater Coast's islands is the world's defining feature — vast, deep, and commanding respect from everyone who…

entries.name: [Location] The Volcanic Island — Cindermark
entries.content: Cindermark is the Dunwater Coast's youngest island — a volcanic peak that is still active, venting steam and occasionally producing flows of lava that reach the sea in spectacular plumes of steam. The island is uninhabited except for a small outpost maintained by observers who track the volcano's activity and warn shipping when conditions become dangerous. The hot springs on Cindermark's lower slopes are a destination for sailors seeking the reputed healing properties of mineral-rich water. Whether the springs heal anything is debatable. Whether soaking in them after weeks at sea feels extraordinary is not. Cindermark is the coast's most dramatic landmark — visible as a plume of steam from miles at sea, a navigational reference and a reminder that the islands themselves are temporary.
entries.description: The Volcanic Island — Cindermark. Cindermark is the Dunwater Coast's youngest island — a volcanic peak that is still active, venting steam and occasionally producing flows…

entries.name: [Location] The Shipwreck Graveyard
entries.content: The Shipwreck Graveyard is a stretch of shallow reef between the outer islands where currents converge and the water hides rock just below the surface. Dozens of wrecks are visible at low tide — masts protruding from the water, hulls broken on the reef, the remains of vessels that miscalculated the passage or were driven onto the rock by storms. The graveyard is a navigational hazard and a salvage opportunity: the wrecks contain cargo, fittings, and timber that the coast's more adventurous sailors retrieve at some risk. The graveyard is also a memorial of sorts — sailors passing through lower their flags and observe a moment of silence for the crews lost here, which is the coast's only formal ritual regarding death at sea.
entries.description: The Shipwreck Graveyard is a stretch of shallow reef between the outer islands where currents converge and the water hides rock just below…

entries.name: [Location] The Lighthouse — Pelican Rock
entries.content: Pelican Rock Lighthouse stands on a lone outcrop at the entrance to Saltmere's bay — a stone tower with a rotating lamp that guides ships through the channel at night and in fog. The lighthouse is maintained by a keeper who lives in the attached quarters and who is, by tradition, someone who has chosen solitude. Keepers serve for years, sometimes decades, tending the lamp and recording weather observations in a logbook that stretches back generations. The logbook is the coast's most complete weather record and is consulted by captains planning voyages. Pelican Rock is the first thing sailors see approaching Saltmere and the last thing they see leaving, which makes the lighthouse keeper the coast's most frequently observed and least frequently spoken to person.
entries.description: The Lighthouse — Pelican Rock. Pelican Rock Lighthouse stands on a lone outcrop at the entrance to Saltmere's bay — a stone tower with a rotating lamp that guides ships…

entries.name: [Location] Hidden Cove — Smuggler's Rest
entries.content: Smuggler's Rest is an inlet on the uninhabited side of Saltmere's island, accessible only through a narrow channel obscured by rock formations. The cove is deep enough to shelter small vessels and sheltered enough to be invisible from the open water. It has been used for centuries to land cargo that cannot pass through customs — a function everyone knows about and no one officially acknowledges. The cove contains a small beach, a few storage structures, and the kind of casual infrastructure that builds up when people use a place regularly without maintaining it formally. Smuggler's Rest is the Dunwater Coast in miniature: a practical arrangement that serves everyone's interests and survives because no one benefits from disrupting it.
entries.description: Hidden Cove — Smuggler's Rest. Smuggler's Rest is an inlet on the uninhabited side of Saltmere's island, accessible only through a narrow channel obscured by rock…

entries.name: [Location] Governor's Island — Whitecliff
entries.content: Whitecliff is the seat of whatever central authority the Dunwater Coast acknowledges — a fortified island with a governor's mansion, a naval yard, and a garrison that is perpetually undermanned. The governor is appointed by a distant nation that claims sovereignty over the coast and exercises it primarily through tariff collection. The garrison patrols the shipping lanes and enforces customs regulations with varying degrees of enthusiasm and corruption. Whitecliff represents the law in the same way that a scarecrow represents a farmer — the shape is right but the substance is absent. The governor's authority is real within Whitecliff's harbor and theoretical everywhere else. The rest of the coast acknowledges this arrangement with the polite fiction that the governor matters more than they actually believe.
entries.description: Governor's Island — Whitecliff. Whitecliff is the seat of whatever central authority the Dunwater Coast acknowledges — a fortified island with a governor's mansion, a…

entries.name: [Location] The Deep Water
entries.content: Beyond the island chains, the ocean floor drops into trenches of unknown depth. The deep water is where the Dunwater Coast's maritime culture meets its limits — fishermen will not fish there, sailors avoid it when possible, and the few who have sailed over the deep trenches report a quality of water that is different: darker, colder at the surface than it should be, and carrying sounds from below that are probably just geological activity and are definitely not discussed in detail. The deep water is the coast's unknown — the place where confidence in charts and experience fails and where the ocean reveals that it is much larger and much older than the people who sail its surface. Fishermen mention the deep water the way they mention storms: with respect and minimal elaboration.
entries.description: The Deep Water. Beyond the island chains, the ocean floor drops into trenches of unknown depth.

entries.name: [Faction] Pirate Crews
entries.content: Pirate crews on the Dunwater Coast are organized enterprises — each with a captain, a hierarchy, a set of operating principles, and a financial arrangement that distributes shares of plunder according to rank and role. Crews range from small fast-attack boats to multi-ship operations that can blockade a port. The best crews are professional: they target profitable cargo, avoid unnecessary violence, and maintain a reputation for reliability that, paradoxically, makes them trusted counterparts in negotiations. The worst crews are violent, unpredictable, and short-lived. The captains' council at Blackpool mediates between crews and enforces the basic rules that keep piracy sustainable. Piracy on the Dunwater Coast is a career, not an adventure, and it is judged by the same standards as any other career: competence, reliability, and results.
entries.description: Pirate crews on the Dunwater Coast are organized enterprises — each with a captain, a hierarchy, a set of operating principles, and a…

entries.name: [Faction] Merchant Guilds
entries.content: Merchant guilds organize the coast's legal trade — or at least the trade that is declared legal at the customs house. Each guild specializes: spice merchants, textile traders, timber haulers, and the general cargo operators who will move anything for the right price. The guilds negotiate rates, coordinate shipping schedules, and maintain the infrastructure of trade — warehouses, dock access, loading equipment. Guild membership provides access to shared intelligence about routes, weather, and pirate activity. The guilds and the pirates exist in a relationship that is competitive and symbiotic: the pirates create the risk that makes escort services valuable, and the merchants create the cargo that makes piracy profitable. Both sides understand this. Neither side discusses it openly.
entries.description: Merchant guilds organize the coast's legal trade — or at least the trade that is declared legal at the customs house.

entries.name: [Faction] Port Magistrates
entries.content: Port magistrates govern the major port towns with a pragmatism that reflects the coast's priorities. A magistrate's primary function is collecting tariffs, which fund the port's infrastructure: docks, warehouses, lighthouse maintenance, and the small constabulary that keeps the peace in town. Magistrates are appointed by the governor on Whitecliff and serve at the governor's pleasure, which in practice means they serve as long as tariff revenue flows and nobody complains too loudly. The best magistrates balance enforcement with flexibility — collecting enough to fund governance without driving trade to unregulated ports. The worst magistrates are corrupt, which is tolerated up to the point where corruption interferes with trade, at which point the merchant guilds apply pressure that the governor finds persuasive.
entries.description: Port magistrates govern the major port towns with a pragmatism that reflects the coast's priorities.

entries.name: [Faction] The Navy
entries.content: The Dunwater Coast's navy operates from Whitecliff with a fleet that is small, aging, and spread too thin to patrol the waters it is responsible for. Naval vessels cruise the major shipping lanes, occasionally engage pirate ships, and spend most of their time showing the flag — a presence that deters some crime and drives the rest to less patrolled waters. Naval officers are a mix of career professionals, political appointees, and people who chose the navy over prison. The sailors are competent because the sea requires competence regardless of institutional quality. The navy's relationship with the coast's other factions is complicated: they are supposed to enforce the law, the law is routinely ignored, and the navy's budget depends on the trade that the law's selective enforcement enables.
entries.description: The Navy. The Dunwater Coast's navy operates from Whitecliff with a fleet that is small, aging, and spread too thin to patrol the waters it is…

entries.name: [Faction] Smuggler Networks
entries.content: Smuggling on the Dunwater Coast is organized into networks that span multiple islands and involve contacts at every level of the coast's society: dockworkers who look the other way, customs officials who miscalculate cargo weights, merchants who maintain two sets of books, and the captains who move the actual goods. The networks are decentralized — no single person controls them, which makes them resistant to disruption. Smuggling handles goods that cannot pass through customs due to tariffs, prohibition, or origin: untaxed spirits, restricted weapons, stolen cargo, and materials from islands that have no trade agreements. The smuggler networks are the coast's shadow economy, and they function because the official economy cannot supply everything the coast's population needs and everyone involved prefers practical solutions to principled shortages.
entries.description: Smuggler Networks. Smuggling on the Dunwater Coast is organized into networks that span multiple islands and involve contacts at every level of the coast's…

entries.name: [Faction] Deep-Water Fishermen
entries.content: Deep-water fishermen are a distinct community within the Dunwater Coast's seafaring population. They sail farther from shore than traders or pirates, into waters where the ocean floor drops away and the catches are larger and stranger. Deep-water fishermen are quieter than other sailors, more superstitious, and more reluctant to discuss what they encounter on the open ocean. They have their own traditions: specific rituals before sailing, taboo words that are not spoken at sea, and a practice of pouring the first catch back as an offering to whatever lives below. These traditions are not religious in any organized sense. They are the accumulated responses of generations of people who have sailed into waters where the familiar ends and something else begins, and who have learned that respect is the safest approach to the unknown.
entries.description: Deep-water fishermen are a distinct community within the Dunwater Coast's seafaring population.

entries.name: [Character] Captain Archetype
entries.content: Captains on the Dunwater Coast are defined by their ships and their crews. A captain's authority is absolute aboard their vessel and meaningless ashore — the transition between the two is handled with varying degrees of grace. Good captains know the water, know their ship, and know their crew well enough to keep all three working together in conditions that range from tedious to lethal. They make decisions quickly because the sea does not wait for consensus. They accept responsibility for those decisions because a captain who blames their crew has already lost their crew's trust. Captains are respected in proportion to their competence, which is measured by a simple metric: is their crew alive and is their cargo delivered. Everything else is conversation.
entries.description: Captain Archetype. Captains on the Dunwater Coast are defined by their ships and their crews.

entries.name: [Character] Navigator Archetype
entries.content: Navigators on the Dunwater Coast are the most technically skilled sailors — people who can read the stars, the currents, the wind, and the color of the water to determine position and plot course. Navigation here relies on a mix of charts that are incomplete, star positions that shift with the season, and accumulated knowledge passed from navigator to navigator. A good navigator is invaluable and knows it — they are among the highest-paid crew members and the most sought after. Their charts, annotated with personal observations, are their most prized possessions and are sometimes more valuable than the ships they guide. Navigators develop an intimate relationship with the sea that other sailors do not share: they understand its patterns at a level that borders on intuition.
entries.description: Navigator Archetype. Navigators on the Dunwater Coast are the most technically skilled sailors — people who can read the stars, the currents, the wind, and the…

entries.name: [Character] Dockworker Archetype
entries.content: Dockworkers are the Dunwater Coast's backbone — the people who load and unload cargo, maintain the docks, and keep the machinery of trade physically moving. They are strong, practical, and deeply familiar with the contents of every crate they handle, which makes them the coast's most comprehensive source of intelligence about what is actually being traded. Dockworkers know which crates are heavier than their manifests claim, which merchants use double bottoms, and which ships carry cargo that was not declared at customs. They share this knowledge selectively and profit from it modestly. A dockworker's loyalty is to the dock and to the other dockworkers — they are a community within the community, and their cooperation is essential to the function of every port.
entries.description: Dockworker Archetype. Dockworkers are the Dunwater Coast's backbone — the people who load and unload cargo, maintain the docks, and keep the machinery of trade…

entries.name: [Character] Smuggler Archetype
entries.content: Smugglers on the Dunwater Coast are pragmatists who have identified the gap between what the law allows and what the market demands and built a career in the space between. They are not romantics — smuggling is logistics with higher stakes and better pay. Good smugglers are invisible: their cargo moves through networks so well-established that the goods arrive without anyone along the chain knowing the full picture. Bad smugglers are caught, and being caught means losing cargo, ship, and freedom. Smugglers cultivate relationships at every level — dock crews, customs officers, harbor masters, and the captains who provide cover. Their social network is their primary asset, and maintaining it requires the same trust-building that legitimate merchants invest in their guilds.
entries.description: Smuggler Archetype. Smugglers on the Dunwater Coast are pragmatists who have identified the gap between what the law allows and what the market demands and…

entries.name: [Character] Magistrate Archetype
entries.content: Magistrates on the Dunwater Coast are administrators first and law enforcers second. They manage ports, collect tariffs, settle disputes, and maintain the infrastructure that keeps trade flowing. A good magistrate understands that their authority depends on being useful — a port that functions brings revenue, and revenue buys the governor's tolerance. Magistrates deal with every faction: merchants who want lower tariffs, pirates who want to be left alone, smugglers who want to be invisible, and the navy which wants to appear effective. Balancing these interests requires diplomacy, flexibility, and the willingness to pretend not to see things that are better left unseen. The best magistrates are those whose ports prosper. How they achieve prosperity is not closely examined.
entries.description: Magistrate Archetype. Magistrates on the Dunwater Coast are administrators first and law enforcers second.

entries.name: [Character] The Old Sailor
entries.content: Every port on the Dunwater Coast has at least one old sailor — someone who has sailed for decades, survived storms that killed better crews, and accumulated knowledge of the water that cannot be taught, only lived. Old sailors are found at dockside taverns, on harbor benches, and at the ends of piers where the fishing is poor but the view of the harbor is good. They tell stories that are seventy percent true, give advice that is ninety percent reliable, and hold opinions about weather that are more accurate than any instrument. Old sailors are the coast's living memory — they remember storms, shipwrecks, famous captains, and the way the water used to behave in places where it now behaves differently. They are also the ones who do not discuss the deep water, which tells visitors more about the deep water than any story would.
entries.description: The Old Sailor. Every port on the Dunwater Coast has at least one old sailor — someone who has sailed for decades, survived storms that killed better…

entries.name: [Mechanics] Sailing and Navigation
entries.content: Sailing the Dunwater Coast requires knowledge of winds, currents, tides, and the specific behavior of water around each island chain. Wind patterns are seasonal and generally reliable. Currents between islands run in established channels that experienced sailors read by water color and surface texture. Navigation is by star, landmark, and chart — charts that are hand-drawn, constantly updated, and never complete. The coast's waters are navigable but not forgiving: reefs lurk beneath surfaces that look safe, currents can push a vessel off course in minutes, and the weather can change from calm to storm faster than a crew can prepare. Good sailing is about reading conditions accurately and responding immediately. The sea rewards attention and punishes assumption.
entries.description: Sailing and Navigation. Sailing the Dunwater Coast requires knowledge of winds, currents, tides, and the specific behavior of water around each island chain.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Ship Combat
entries.content: Combat at sea on the Dunwater Coast is fast, violent, and decided by positioning rather than firepower. Most engagements begin with one vessel pursuing another and end when the faster ship escapes or the slower ship is boarded. Cannons exist but are expensive, heavy, and consumed by ammunition that is difficult to replace. Most combat relies on boarding — closing the distance, grappling, and fighting hand-to-hand on a moving deck. Boarding actions favor the aggressive: the attacking crew chooses when and where to engage, and the defending crew must react. The best pirates win through intimidation — flying a recognized flag and relying on the target's calculation that surrendering cargo is cheaper than fighting. The worst pirates win through violence, which works until it attracts a coordinated response.
entries.description: Ship Combat. Combat at sea on the Dunwater Coast is fast, violent, and decided by positioning rather than firepower.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Weather and Storms
entries.content: Weather on the Dunwater Coast is the constant negotiation between warm water, unstable air, and the open ocean's capacity to generate violence from calm. Storms arrive fast — a clear morning can become a howling squall by noon, driven by temperature differentials that create wind speeds capable of dismasting a ship. The major storm season runs for three months and produces hurricanes that can destroy harbors and reshape coastlines. During storm season, smart captains stay in port, and smart magistrates stock supplies. Between storms, the weather is warm, breezy, and deceptively pleasant. The coast's relationship with weather is one of constant awareness: sailors check the sky before they check their cargo, and the horizon is monitored not for land but for the specific darkening that means a storm is building.
entries.description: Weather and Storms. Weather on the Dunwater Coast is the constant negotiation between warm water, unstable air, and the open ocean's capacity to generate…

entries.name: [Mechanics] Law at Sea vs Law on Land
entries.content: Law on the Dunwater Coast operates on two systems that intersect but do not align. On land, port magistrates enforce customs regulations, settle disputes, and maintain order using whatever authority the governor has delegated. At sea, there is no law except what captains enforce on their own vessels and what the navy can project from its limited fleet. The boundary is the waterline, and crossing it changes the rules entirely. A merchant who is law-abiding in Saltmere may carry undeclared cargo once outside the harbor. A pirate who raids shipping lanes may walk freely in Blackpool. The dual system creates a culture of compartmentalization: people behave according to where they are rather than who they are, and identity becomes situational in a way that the coast considers normal and outsiders find disorienting.
entries.description: Law at Sea vs Law on Land. Law on the Dunwater Coast operates on two systems that intersect but do not align.

entries.name: [Mechanics] The Things Below
entries.content: The deep water around the Dunwater Coast contains things. This is stated as fact by fishermen, acknowledged by sailors, and left deliberately vague by everyone. The things below are glimpsed rather than observed: a shadow beneath a hull that is too large to be a whale and moves against the current. Nets pulled up from deep water containing fish with bite marks from mouths wider than any known species. The occasional surfacing of something massive, seen at distance, that dives before details can be confirmed. Deep-water fishermen have the most experience with these phenomena and the least willingness to describe them. What is known: the deep water is inhabited by something large, possibly multiple somethings, that does not interfere with surface activity as long as surface activity does not interfere with it. This arrangement is maintained by mutual avoidance and the fishermen's tradition of giving back the first catch.
entries.description: The Things Below. The deep water around the Dunwater Coast contains things.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Shipbuilding and Repair
entries.content: Ships on the Dunwater Coast are built and repaired at shipyards in the major ports — Saltmere has the largest, Blackpool has the most discreet. Shipbuilding uses local timber from the jungle-covered islands, treated with techniques that resist salt damage and marine growth. A ship's hull is its most important component, and hull maintenance is continuous: scraping, caulking, and repair happen at every port stop. The coast's shipwrights are among its most valued craftspeople, and their skills are passed through apprenticeships that last years. A well-built ship can last decades with proper maintenance. A poorly built ship announces its quality to the ocean, and the ocean responds accordingly. The shipyards also serve as intelligence networks — the condition and modification of a vessel tells its story to anyone who knows how to read it.
entries.description: Shipbuilding and Repair. Ships on the Dunwater Coast are built and repaired at shipyards in the major ports — Saltmere has the largest, Blackpool has the most…

entries.name: [Concept] The Sea Lets You Start Over
entries.content: The Dunwater Coast's most valuable commodity is not spice or timber or stolen cargo — it is the opportunity to become someone new. The sea erases. Every mile of water between you and your past is a mile of forgetting, and the coast has miles to spare. People arrive with histories they do not discuss, names they chose that morning, and skills that suggest previous lives more complex than their current stories account for. The coast accepts this because the coast was built by people doing exactly the same thing. Identity here is performed rather than inherited — you are who you demonstrate yourself to be, and the demonstration is continuous. This produces a culture of strange honesty: people lie about their pasts and are truthful about everything else, because on the coast, what you did yesterday matters less than what you will do today.
entries.description: The Sea Lets You Start Over. The Dunwater Coast's most valuable commodity is not spice or timber or stolen cargo — it is the opportunity to become someone new.

entries.name: [Concept] Pirate vs Merchant
entries.content: The distinction between pirate and merchant on the Dunwater Coast is largely a matter of perspective, current activity, and who is telling the story. A merchant who acquires cargo from a pirate is a merchant. A pirate who sells stolen cargo through a merchant is a pirate. A captain who does both, depending on the season and the opportunity, is the coast's most common archetype. The line between piracy and trade is blurred by economic reality: the same captain may trade legitimately in Saltmere, carry undeclared cargo between ports, and seize an opportunity at sea if the risk-reward calculation favors it. The coast does not judge this flexibility. It understands that survival here requires adapting to conditions, and conditions at sea change faster than morality can follow.
entries.description: Pirate vs Merchant. The distinction between pirate and merchant on the Dunwater Coast is largely a matter of perspective, current activity, and who is telling…

entries.name: [Concept] What the Fishermen Won't Talk About
entries.content: Deep-water fishermen on the Dunwater Coast observe a collective silence about certain subjects. They do not describe what they see in the deep water in specific terms. They do not name the things they have glimpsed beneath the surface. They do not discuss the occasions when nets come up damaged in ways that do not match any known marine life. This silence is not superstition — it is practice, developed over generations by people who have learned that the deep water responds to attention, and that describing what lives there constitutes a form of attention they would rather not give. The fishermen's silence is the coast's most eloquent communication: what they will not say tells listeners more than what they would say, and the telling is in the refusal, and the refusal is absolute.
entries.description: What the Fishermen Won't Talk About. Deep-water fishermen on the Dunwater Coast observe a collective silence about certain subjects.

entries.name: [Concept] Salt as Culture
entries.content: Salt is the Dunwater Coast's defining substance. It is in the air, on every surface, in the food, on the skin. Salt preserves fish, seasons meals, and crusts the rigging of every ship. The taste of salt is the first thing visitors notice and the last thing residents think about — it has become ambient, invisible through familiarity, the baseline flavor of every experience. Salt is also metaphorical: the coast's culture is salt-cured, preserved against decay by the constant exposure to the ocean's influence. Things that last on the coast are things that can withstand salt — ships, relationships, reputations. Things that cannot are eroded, steadily and without malice, until they dissolve. Salt is patient. The coast is patient. The relationship between the two is the same as the relationship between the coast and everything else: what endures, endures.
entries.description: Salt as Culture. Salt is the Dunwater Coast's defining substance. It is in the air, on every surface, in the food, on the skin.

entries.name: [Concept] The Horizon at Sea
entries.content: The horizon at sea is different from the horizon on land. On land, it is a line where earth meets sky. At sea, it is a circle — you are at the center of visible water in every direction, and the edge is equidistant everywhere. This creates a sensation of being exactly in the middle of everything and nowhere at all simultaneously. Sailors develop a relationship with the sea horizon that shapes their psychology: they become comfortable with uncertainty, with the knowledge that what lies beyond sight is unknown and that the distance to any destination is approximate until you arrive. The sea horizon teaches patience because it cannot be hurried. It teaches humility because it is always the same distance away. It teaches the Dunwater Coast's most important lesson: the world is larger than you, and it does not require your understanding to continue.
entries.description: The horizon at sea is different from the horizon on land. On land, it is a line where earth meets sky.

entries.name: [NPC] Magistra Fenn — Port Magistrate of Saltmere
entries.content:
Magistra Fenn governs Saltmere with the pragmatic efficiency of someone who understands that her authority extends exactly as far as trade revenue justifies. She is sharp, well-dressed by port standards, and conducts business with a directness that intimidates people accustomed to political maneuvering. Fenn collects tariffs fairly, resolves disputes quickly, and maintains the deliberate fiction that all cargo passing through Saltmere is properly declared. She is not corrupt — she is realistic, and realism on the Dunwater Coast means knowing which battles to fight and which to invoice.

entries.name: [NPC] Corsair — Pirate Captain
entries.content:
Corsair is a pirate captain operating out of Blackpool — a broad-shouldered woman with sun-darkened skin and a voice that carries across a deck in a gale. She commands through competence and the specific kind of charisma that comes from being the most capable person on any ship she boards. Corsair's crew is loyal because she pays fair shares and keeps them alive. Her reputation is professional: she targets profitable cargo, avoids unnecessary violence, and honors ship-to-ship agreements. She is feared and respected in equal measure, which on the Dunwater Coast is the same thing.
<BunnymoTags>Corsair: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:38>, <HAIRCOLOR:Black>, <EYECOLOR:Dark>, <BUILD:Muscular>, <DERE:Oujidere>, <ATTACHMENT:Avoidant>, <TRAIT:Commanding>, <TRAIT:Professional>, <TRAIT:Bold>, <MBTI:ESTP-A>, <BOUNDARIES:FIRM>, <TRUST:PROVEN>, <LOYALTY:Crew>, <DECISION:Instinctive></BunnymoTags>

entries.name: [NPC] Tallow — Smuggler Network Contact
entries.content:
Tallow operates at the intersection of legal and illegal trade — a thin, quiet man who can be found at the Saltmere docks most evenings, watching ships arrive and making mental notes about cargo weights. He arranges things: shipments that bypass customs, meetings between parties who cannot be seen meeting, and solutions to problems that the official economy cannot solve. Tallow never handles cargo himself. He connects people who need things with people who have things, takes a percentage, and ensures both parties are satisfied. His network is his only asset, and he maintains it with the care of someone whose life depends on it, because it does.
<BunnymoTags>Tallow: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:35>, <HAIRCOLOR:Sandy>, <EYECOLOR:Grey>, <BUILD:Thin>, <DERE:Kuudere>, <ATTACHMENT:Avoidant>, <TRAIT:Discreet>, <TRAIT:Connected>, <TRAIT:Calculated>, <MBTI:INTP-A>, <BOUNDARIES:PROFESSIONAL>, <TRUST:MUTUAL>, <DECISION:Risk_Assessed>, <MASK:Unremarkable></BunnymoTags>

entries.name: [NPC] Brine — Old Fisherman
entries.content:
Brine has fished the Dunwater Coast for fifty years and his body shows it — salt-weathered skin, hands like rope, and eyes that have stared at the ocean long enough to see things he does not discuss. He sits at the end of the Saltmere pier most evenings, mending nets and offering advice to anyone who asks and some who don't. Brine's knowledge of the water is unmatched — currents, weather, fish behavior, and the specific quality of deep water that tells an experienced sailor to turn back. He does not talk about what he has seen in the deep trenches. He changes the subject with a skill that is itself informative.
<BunnymoTags>Brine: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:68>, <HAIRCOLOR:White>, <EYECOLOR:Faded Blue>, <BUILD:Weathered>, <DERE:Oujidere>, <ATTACHMENT:Avoidant>, <TRAIT:Wise>, <TRAIT:Guarded>, <TRAIT:Generous>, <MBTI:ISTP-A>, <BOUNDARIES:SELECTIVE>, <TRUST:SLOW>, <MASK:Simple></BunnymoTags>
